request.question: Who was the 2nd year head coach of the team who played against Florida State in the 101st Rose Bowl?

title: 1976 Rose Bowl
1995 Rose Bowl
1975 Rose Bowl
1965 Rose Bowl
2013 Michigan State Spartans football team
1954 UCLA Bruins football team
J.K. Kennedy
502nd Infantry Regiment (United States)
2014 Oregon Ducks football team
2015 Rose Bowl
sentences: The 1976 Rose Bowl was a college football bowl game played on January 1, 1976.
It was the 62nd Rose Bowl Game.
The UCLA Bruins defeated the number one ranked and undefeated Ohio State Buckeyes 23–10 in a rare bowl rematch of a regular season game.
UCLA quarterback John Sciarra was named the Rose Bowl Player Of The Game.

This was the first post season that football teams from the Big Ten Conference and Pacific-10 Conference could appear in other bowl games since the Rose Bowl agreement was signed for the 1947 Rose Bowl.
This game marked Ohio State coach Woody Hayes' last appearance in the Rose Bowl game.
The 1995 Rose Bowl was a college football bowl game played on Monday January 2, 1995, because New Year's Day was on a Sunday.
It was the 81st Rose Bowl Game.
The Penn State Nittany Lions defeated the Oregon Ducks 38–20.
Ki-Jana Carter of Penn State and Danny O'Neil of Oregon both were named the Rose Bowl Players of the Game.
Many observers point to this game as the one that made Ki-Jana Carter a number one selection in the 1995 NFL Draft.
The 1975 Rose Bowl was a college football bowl game played on January 1, 1975.
It was the 61st Rose Bowl Game.
Fifth ranked USC Trojans defeated third ranked Ohio State Buckeyes 18-17 in one of the most exciting games in the history of the Rose Bowl.
USC quarterback Pat Haden passed to Shelton Diggs for the two-point conversion that gave the Trojans the Rose Bowl victory and the UPI's college football national title.
The 1965 Rose Bowl, played on January 1, 1965, was the 51st Rose Bowl Game.
The Michigan Wolverines defeated the Oregon State Beavers by a score of 34–7.
Michigan fullback Mel Anthony was named the Rose Bowl Player Of The Game.
The 2013 Michigan State Spartans football team represented Michigan State University in the Legends Division of the Big Ten Conference during the 2013 NCAA Division I FBS football season.
Michigan State played their home games at Spartan Stadium in East Lansing, Michigan and were led by seventh year head coach Mark Dantonio.
The Spartans finished the year 13–1, 8–0 and undefeated in Big Ten play.
By winning the Legends Division, they earned a trip to the Big Ten Championship Game, their second appearance in the championship game.
Facing No. 2-ranked Ohio State, they defeated the Buckeyes 34–24, knocking Ohio State out of the running for the BCS National Championship Game.
The Spartans received an invitation to the Rose Bowl, their first trip to the Rose Bowl since 1988.
There they defeated No. 5-ranked Stanford for the school's first Rose Bowl win since 1988.
The 1954 UCLA Bruins football team represented the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) in the 1954 college football season.
They played their home games at the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum and were coached by Henry Russell Sanders.
It was Sanders' sixth season as the UCLA head coach.
The Bruins finished 9–0 overall, and were Pacific Coast Conference Champions with a 6–0 record.
The Bruins compiled 367 points against their opponents in nine games, and allowed only 40 points.
The Bruins were not eligible to play in the Rose Bowl vs. Ohio State because of the "no repeat" rule in effect at the time.
No team was allowed to participate in consecutive Rose Bowl games, and since UCLA had played in the 1954 Rose Bowl, they were excluded from the 1955 event.
USC competed in the Rose Bowl instead, losing to Ohio State.
Following the outcome, UCLA and Ohio State split the national championship since UCLA was ranked #1 by the UPI Coaches Poll and Ohio State was ranked #1 by the AP Poll.
Jesse Kennard "Bud" Kennedy was Florida State men's basketball head coach from 1949 to 1966.
He was born on April 22, 1907 in Lawrence, Kansas.
His father, A. R. Kennedy, was head coach of the Kansas football team.
Kennedy was an athlete at University of Kansas, Baker University, and Kansas State Teachers College.
In 1948 he became the Florida State Seminoles Athletic Manager and Assistant head coach.
The following year, he became the head coach.
Kennedy went 234–208 in his 18 years at Florida State and won the Florida Intercollegiate Conference Championship in 1955.
In 1966, on June, 26 he died of cancer.
Kennedy was 59.
Later in 1977 he was inducted into the Florida State University Hall of Fame.
The 502nd Infantry Regiment (502nd INF), previously titled the 502nd Parachute Infantry Regiment (502nd PIR), is an airborne infantry regiment of the United States Army.

The regiment was established shortly after the American entry into World War II, and was assigned as a regiment of the 101st Airborne Division, "The Screaming Eagles", one of the most decorated formations of the U.S. Army.
The regiment saw substantial action in the European Theater of World War II and was deactivated in 1945, shortly after the end of the war.

Reactivating in a new form in 1956, the 502nd Infantry has served in the Vietnam War, the first Gulf War, Operation Iraqi Freedom, Operation Enduring Freedom in Afghanistan, and Operation Inherent Resolve in Iraq.
It was removed from parachute status with the rest of the 101st Airborne Division in 1969, and since 1974, the regiment has been classified as an Air Assault unit.
Currently, its 1st and 2nd battalions are active.
Both battalions are assigned to the 2nd Brigade Combat Team, 101st Airborne Division (Air Assault).
The 2014 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon in the 2014 NCAA Division I FBS football season.
The team was led by second-year head coach Mark Helfrich and played their home games at Autzen Stadium for the 48th straight year.
They were a member of the Pac-12 Conference in the North Division.

The 2015 Rose Bowl (officially known as the College Football Playoff Semifinal at the Rose Bowl Game presented by Northwestern Mutual) was a college football bowl game played on January 1, 2015, at the Rose Bowl stadium in Pasadena, California.

This 101st Rose Bowl Game, as a semifinal for the College Football Playoff (CFP), matched the Oregon Ducks against the Florida State Seminoles as selected by the system's selection committee to compete for a spot at the National Championship game to be played on January 12, 2015 at AT&T Stadium in Arlington, Texas.
It was one of the 2014–15 bowl games that concluded the 2014 FBS football season.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

The `summary` must be a concise (typically 1–4 short sentences or clauses), self-contained intermediate state that helps the next agent locate the missing second-hop evidence. Do not try to produce the final answer. Follow these rules when composing the `summary`:

1) First-hop evidence: In one short clause, paraphrase or quote (<=15 words) the specific fact from the passages that is relevant to the question and that you are using as the bridge. Include the passage title or identifier that contains this evidence.

2) Bridge / missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity or the specific missing fact that must be resolved in the next hop (for example: an ambiguous given name that must be identified, the named person whose birthplace is required, the tournament title whose venues must be counted, or which film credit remains unverified). If the passages already give a full name, use that full name as the bridge entity; if only a given name or ambiguous identifier is present, state that the identity is unresolved.

3) Retrieval direction: Provide one concise, actionable next-hop retrieval plan (1–3 ordered steps or 1–2 short search-query templates). Use clear placeholders where appropriate (e.g., "fetch the biography page for <NAME>", "search \"<NAME> birthplace\" site:wikipedia.org", "open the '<TOURNAMENT>' page and inspect its Venues/Matches/Schedule section", or "fetch '<FILM>' cast list and compare to '<OTHER FILM>' cast"). Specify which page/title or which page sections the next agent should inspect. Do not perform retrieval or assert any facts that are not present in the given passages.

4) Disambiguation: If the question's referent is ambiguous (e.g., only a given name is provided), request the user's clarification in one short sentence only (e.g., "If you mean a different Maurice, please provide the full name."). Do not ask permission to search; just state the needed clarification.

5) Constraints and tone: Never invent or assert unverified facts (no guessed winners, years, birthplaces, counts, or cast overlaps). Do not output the final answer. Keep language neutral, concise, and actionable. Avoid long verbatim extracts beyond a short supporting quote.

Output exactly one field: `summary` (a single string following the above structure).
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `summary_1`, produce the field `query`.

Output requirements
- Return exactly one compact, single-line retrieval query string in the field `query`. Do not return any other fields, commentary, punctuation, quotes, or the answer.
- Keep the query short and retrieval-oriented (keyword bag, typically ~3–7 meaningful tokens). Prefer tokens already present in `question` or `summary_1`.

Rules for composing the query
1) Nonprofit medical-practice/facility questions: If the question asks about a non-profit/nonprofit medical practice or facility (or uses similar wording) and `summary_1` contains an organization name, do NOT repeat that organization name. Instead produce a concise bridge query combining the place token from the question, the state/region token, and a class term such as "nonprofit medical practice" or "clinic". Use the template: <place> <state> nonprofit medical practice clinic

2) Avoid hypothesizing names: If the needed facility or organization name is not supported by `question` or `summary_1`, do not invent or assert candidate names. Reuse the topic and the expressed information need (topic + class + location) to form the query.

3) Comparative person-role questions: If the question compares two people (e.g., which person fills a larger variety of roles) and the inputs name both persons or `summary_1` lists one person's roles, produce one compact query that names both people and includes concise role/occupation keywords (examples: director producer composer musician writer actor). You may include a brief source token such as Wikipedia to guide retrieval, but remain compact. Do not ask for the final comparison or return conclusions.

General prohibitions
- Do not output full sentences, long explanatory text, lists of candidate organizations, or claims/answers.
- Do not add metadata, instructions, or extra fields—only the single query string.

Produce the `query` field now according to the rules above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, and `passages`, produce the field `summary` (string). Preserve the native input/output interface exactly and return only the `summary` field. Do not produce any other fields, headers, or meta-commentary.

Task: Integrate the newly retrieved `passages` with the existing `context` so the result is a grounded, post-retrieval state that the final-answer agent can use. Keep the summary concise, factual, and limited to information present in `question`, `context`, and `passages`.

Required behavior (apply the relevant rules to each input):
- Preserve facts already asserted in `context` unless the `passages` contradict them. If passages contradict a contextual claim, state the contradiction briefly and retain uncertainty rather than inventing a resolution.
- When a passage (or passages) explicitly supports a claim found in `context`, do all of the following in the `summary`:
  1) Briefly restate the claim from `context` in one short clause.
  2) Identify which retrieved passage(s) provide explicit support by naming the passage title (or a short identifier) in parentheses and give a one-line paraphrase or at most one short quoted fragment drawn directly from that passage as corroboration. Keep paraphrases short and avoid long verbatim spans.
  3) If other passage(s) do not state the claim but supply useful metadata (for example: title, year, director, occupation, full name/alias, location details), list those retained metadata facts in one short clause per passage.
  4) Finish with one concise integration sentence that synthesizes the evidence: state which passage explicitly supports the claim and which passage(s) only supply metadata for downstream use. Resolve the earlier bridge (i.e., commit to the claim) only if explicit support is present; otherwise keep the summary phrased to reflect remaining uncertainty.
- If a passage links two entities (e.g., a facility name to an address or locality) and that link resolves a location or relationship asserted in `context`, include a single short paraphrase that records that linking fact and then a brief integrated sentence that ties that link back to the studio/person/film in question.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Output must be concise (about 1–4 sentences), factual, and limited to content present in the inputs. Prefer 1–3 sentences when the integration is simple.
- Do not add new facts, speculation, or external knowledge beyond `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
- Avoid long quotations; at most one short quoted fragment per supporting passage and only when it directly helps attribute support.
- Do not use hedging language when the passages state facts plainly. Use hedging only when the inputs are ambiguous or contradictory.
- Do not include dataset labels, internal notes, evaluation, or extraneous formatting. Do not produce the final answer outside this `summary` field.

Goal: deliver a compact, evidence-attributed summary that (a) restates the first-hop claim when present, (b) explicitly attributes which passage(s) support that claim and which provide metadata, and (c) synthesizes the evidence so the downstream final-answer agent can use it to answer the question.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional conservative, evidence-driven normalizations and tie-breaking rules (small, shared improvements):
- Use summary_2 as the later integrated evidence and give it priority when summaries disagree about the answer, but do not ignore an explicit short named-entity asserted clearly in summary_1: if either summary contains a concise explicit attribution (for example, phrasing like "directed by <NAME>", "headquartered in <PLACE>", or a direct statement that names the immediate jurisdiction), accept that explicit short named-entity as the supported minimal answer unless the other summary gives a different explicit short named-entity (in that conflicting-explicit case, emit a brief clarification question). In short: prefer explicit concise assertions wherever present; otherwise prefer summary_2 when resolving conflicts.
- When the two summaries agree exactly on a short named phrase that includes a trailing parenthetical disambiguator or trailing parenthetical abbreviation (final ' ( ... )'), strip that single final parenthetical and emit the pre-parenthesis core name (trim surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. Similarly, when the identical agreed phrase ends with the exact suffix ", United States", remove that trailing ", United States" and emit the remaining shorter place string.
- More generally, when an agreed short answer contains trailing clarifying material (a final parentheses block, a trailing parenthetical abbreviation, or an appositive beginning with a comma) and the core name up to the first '(' or the first comma appears verbatim in both summaries, emit that core substring rather than the longer qualified form.
- If the question explicitly requests a size (phrases like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size') and both summaries are identical and contain a phrase of the form '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less', emit only the '<size>' portion (number plus unit) verbatim as the minimal answer (remove the trailing inclusive qualifier).
- If the chosen minimal answer candidate is a contiguous digits-only token sequence (regex ^\d+$), convert it to its spelled-out English words (lowercase, with conventional hyphenation) and emit that word-form as the answer.
- For 'what do X and Y have in common' style questions where both summaries contain the same plural noun token as the shared attribute, emit the singular form of that noun (apply simple regular singularization rules such as removing a trailing 's' or converting 'ies' -> 'y').
- Always prefer a concise explicit short named-entity present verbatim in either summary over asking for clarification when the other summary merely reports inability or uncertainty; however, if the summaries provide two different explicit names for the same field, emit a concise clarification question rather than guessing.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

sentences: The 1976 Rose Bowl was a college football bowl game played on January 1, 1976.
It was the 62nd Rose Bowl Game.
The UCLA Bruins defeated the number one ranked and undefeated Ohio State Buckeyes 23–10 in a rare bowl rematch of a regular season game.
UCLA quarterback John Sciarra was named the Rose Bowl Player Of The Game.

This was the first post season that football teams from the Big Ten Conference and Pacific-10 Conference could appear in other bowl games since the Rose Bowl agreement was signed for the 1947 Rose Bowl.
This game marked Ohio State coach Woody Hayes' last appearance in the Rose Bowl game.

It was the 81st Rose Bowl Game.
The Penn State Nittany Lions defeated the Oregon Ducks 38–20.
Ki-Jana Carter of Penn State and Danny O'Neil of Oregon both were named the Rose Bowl Players of the Game.
Many observers point to this game as the one that made Ki-Jana Carter a number one selection in the 1995 NFL Draft.

The 2013 Michigan State Spartans football team represented Michigan State University in the Legends Division of the Big Ten Conference during the 2013 NCAA Division I FBS football season.
Michigan State played their home games at Spartan Stadium in East Lansing, Michigan and were led by seventh year head coach Mark Dantonio.
The Spartans finished the year 13–1, 8–0 and undefeated in Big Ten play.
By winning the Legends Division, they earned a trip to the Big Ten Championship Game, their second appearance in the championship game.
Facing No. 2-ranked Ohio State, they defeated the Buckeyes 34–24, knocking Ohio State out of the running for the BCS National Championship Game.
The Spartans received an invitation to the Rose Bowl, their first trip to the Rose Bowl since 1988.
There they defeated No. 5-ranked Stanford for the school's first Rose Bowl win since 1988.
The 1954 UCLA Bruins football team represented the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) in the 1954 college football season.
They played their home games at the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum and were coached by Henry Russell Sanders.
It was Sanders' sixth season as the UCLA head coach.
The Bruins finished 9–0 overall, and were Pacific Coast Conference Champions with a 6–0 record.
The Bruins compiled 367 points against their opponents in nine games, and allowed only 40 points.
The Bruins were not eligible to play in the Rose Bowl vs. Ohio State because of the "no repeat" rule in effect at the time.
No team was allowed to participate in consecutive Rose Bowl games, and since UCLA had played in the 1954 Rose Bowl, they were excluded from the 1955 event.
USC competed in the Rose Bowl instead, losing to Ohio State.
Following the outcome, UCLA and Ohio State split the national championship since UCLA was ranked #1 by the UPI Coaches Poll and Ohio State was ranked #1 by the AP Poll.

He was born on April 22, 1907 in Lawrence, Kansas.

Kennedy was an athlete at University of Kansas, Baker University, and Kansas State Teachers College.

The 502nd Infantry Regiment (502nd INF), previously titled the 502nd Parachute Infantry Regiment (502nd PIR), is an airborne infantry regiment of the United States Army.

The regiment was established shortly after the American entry into World War II, and was assigned as a regiment of the 101st Airborne Division, "The Screaming Eagles", one of the most decorated formations of the U.S. Army.
The regiment saw substantial action in the European Theater of World War II and was deactivated in 1945, shortly after the end of the war.

It was removed from parachute status with the rest of the 101st Airborne Division in 1969, and since 1974, the regiment has been classified as an Air Assault unit.

Both battalions are assigned to the 2nd Brigade Combat Team, 101st Airborne Division (Air Assault).

The team was led by second-year head coach Mark Helfrich and played their home games at Autzen Stadium for the 48th straight year.
They were a member of the Pac-12 Conference in the North Division.

response.hop1_query: Who was the 2nd year head coach of the team who played against Florida State in the 101st Rose Bowl?
hop1_docs: 1957 Stanford Indians football team | The 1957 Stanford Indians football team represented Stanford University in the 1957 college football season.  The team was coached by Chuck Taylor in his seventh year as head coach.  Taylor was a popular coach who had led the Indians to the 1952 Rose Bowl and who had been an All-American player for the Indians who starred on the undefeated 1940 "Wow Boys" team that won the 1941 Rose Bowl and the national championship in several polls.  Just before the team's final game against archrival California, Taylor announced he was retiring as head coach to become assistant athletic director of the university.

1999 Michigan State Spartans football team | The 1999 Michigan State Spartans football team represented Michigan State University in the 1999 NCAA Division I-A football season season.  The Spartans played their home games at Spartan Stadium in East Lansing, Michigan.  This was the last year for head coach Nick Saban, who left the program on December 5 to take the head coaching position at Louisiana State.  During the bowl game, the Spartans were coached by interim head coach Bobby Williams, who led the Spartans to a 37–34 victory in the 2000 Florida Citrus Bowl over the Florida Gators of the Southeastern Conference, with a last second, game-winning field goal by kicker Paul Edinger.

2015 Rose Bowl | The 2015 Rose Bowl (officially known as the College Football Playoff Semifinal at the Rose Bowl Game presented by Northwestern Mutual) was a college football bowl game played on January 1, 2015, at the Rose Bowl stadium in Pasadena, California.  This 101st Rose Bowl Game, as a semifinal for the College Football Playoff (CFP), matched the Oregon Ducks against the Florida State Seminoles as selected by the system's selection committee to compete for a spot at the National Championship game to be played on January 12, 2015 at AT&T Stadium in Arlington, Texas.  It was one of the 2014–15 bowl games that concluded the 2014 FBS football season.

2016 South Florida Bulls football team | The 2016 South Florida Bulls football team represented the University of South Florida (USF) in the 2016 NCAA Division I FBS football season.  The 2016 season is the 20th season for the Bulls, and their fourth as a member of the American Athletic Conference (The American).  They played their home games at Raymond James Stadium in Tampa, Florida, and were led during the regular season by head coach Willie Taggart, who was in his fourth year.  Following the regular season, but before the team's appearance in the Birmingham Bowl, Taggart left to take the head coaching vacancy at Oregon, with co-offensive coordinator T. J. Weist named as interim head coach for the bowl game.

1986 Indiana Hoosiers football team | The 1986 Indiana Hoosiers football team represented Indiana University during the 1986 NCAA Division I-A football season.  They participated as members of the Big Ten Conference.  The Hoosiers played their home games at Memorial Stadium in Bloomington, Indiana.  The team was led by Bill Mallory, who was serving in his third year as head coach of the Hoosiers.  The Hoosiers participated in the All-American Bowl against Florida State, losing 13–27.

1984 Rose Bowl | The 1984 Rose Bowl game, played on January 2, was the 70th Rose Bowl Game.  The UCLA Bruins defeated the Illinois Fighting Illini by a score of 45–9.  Rick Neuheisel, UCLA quarterback, was named the Rose Bowl Player Of The Game.  He completed 22 of 32 passes for 298 yards and four touchdowns. Neuheisel, who was named head coach of Bruins in December 2007, threw two touchdown passes to his eventual predecessor as Bruin head coach, Karl Dorrell.  The game was played on January 2, as New Year's Day fell on a Sunday.

1996 Ohio State Buckeyes football team | The 1996 Ohio State Buckeyes football team represented the Ohio State University in the 1996 NCAA Division I-A football season.  The team's head coach was John Cooper.  The Buckeyes played their home games in Ohio Stadium.  The team finished the season with a win-loss record of 11–1, and a Big Ten Conference record of 7–1.  They were co-champions of Big Ten Conference with the Northwestern Wildcats and played in the 1997 Rose Bowl against Pacific-10 Confernece champion the Arizona State Sun Devils.  Ohio State did not play Northwestern during the regular season and were selected to play in the Rose Bowl due to their better overall record of 10–1 (the Wildcats were 9–2).  Northwestern had played in the previous Rose Bowl.
response.summary_1: First-hop: "The 101st Rose Bowl matched the Oregon Ducks against the Florida State Seminoles" (2015 Rose Bowl). Bridge/missing fact: the identity of the Oregon Ducks' head coach during that season (who was in his 2nd year) is not given in the passages. Retrieval direction: 1) Open the "2014 Oregon Ducks football team" (or "2015 Rose Bowl" — Oregon team section) page and inspect the infobox fields "Head coach" and "Hc year"; if needed 2) open the head coach's biography page (e.g., "Mark Helfrich") and check the head-coach tenure/start year. Disambiguation: none needed.
hop2_docs: 2014 Oregon Ducks football team | The 2014 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon in the 2014 NCAA Division I FBS football season.  The team was led by second-year head coach Mark Helfrich and played their home games at Autzen Stadium for the 48th straight year.  They were a member of the Pac-12 Conference in the North Division.

2009 Oregon Ducks football team | The 2009 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon in the 2009 NCAA Division I FBS football season.  The team was led by head coach Chip Kelly in his first season as a head coach at the Division I FBS level.  Kelly was only the third Ducks head coach since 1977 and led the Ducks to a Pac-10 Championship and was named Pac-10 Coach of the Year.  He took over for Mike Bellotti.

2014–15 Oregon Ducks women's basketball team | The 2014–15 Oregon Ducks women's basketball team represented the University of Oregon during the 2014–15 NCAA Division I women's basketball season.  The Ducks, led by first year head coach Kelly Graves, played their games at the Matthew Knight Arena and were members of the Pac-12 Conference.  They finished the season 13–17, 6–12 in Pac-12 play to finish in a tie for ninth place.  They lost in the first round in the Pac-12 Women's Tournament to Washington State.

1995 Oregon Ducks football team | The 1995 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon during the 1995 NCAA Division I-A football season.  They were led by head coach Mike Bellotti, who was in his 1st season as head coach of the Ducks after replacing Rich Brooks, who resigned in February 1995 to become the head coach of the St. Louis Rams.  They played their home games at Autzen Stadium in Eugene, Oregon and participated as members of the Pacific-10 Conference.

Oregon Ducks softball | The Oregon Ducks softball team represents the University of Oregon in NCAA Division I college softball in the Pac-12 Conference.  The head coach is Mike White who is entering his 8th season with the Ducks.  Oregon will now play home games at Jane Sanders Stadium after playing at Howe Field through 2015.  Oregon has appeared in seven Women's College World Series, in 1976, 1980, 1989, 2012, 2014, 2015 and 2017.

1991 Oregon Ducks football team | The 1991 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon during the 1991 NCAA Division I-A football season.  They were led by head coach Rich Brooks, who was in his 15th season as head coach of the Ducks.  They played their home games at Autzen Stadium in Eugene, Oregon and participated as members of the Pacific-10 Conference.

1998 Oregon Ducks football team | The 1998 Oregon Ducks football team represented the University of Oregon during the 1998 NCAA Division I-A football season.  They were led by head coach Mike Bellotti, who was in his 4th season as head coach of the Ducks.  They played their home games at Autzen Stadium in Eugene, Oregon and participated as members of the Pacific-10 Conference.
